item.title: A Guided Fusion Network based on Cross-Scale Semantic Alignment for multi-spectral object detection
item.dc:creator: Hongtao Wu
Jing Rong
Qiuzhan Zhou
Di Yang
Weiqi Wang
item.description: Multi-spectral object detection gains significant attention due to its potential in all-weather applications, particularly those involving RGB and Infrared (IR) images. However, existing methods fail to account for the semantic differences between the RGB and IR modalities, resulting in insufficient modal feature fusion and limiting the performance of RGB-IR object detection. To address these issues, we propose a novel learning network, the Semantic Alignment Guided Fusion Network (SAGF-Net), that fuses RGB and IR images in a guided manner to extract more complementary feature representations. Our network consists of three key modules: Cross-Scale Semantic Alignment (CSSA), Cross-Modal Information Guidance (CMIG), and Deformable Cross-Attention (DCA). The CSSA module alleviates semantic differences across different receptive fields through cross-scale dynamic attention fusion. Subsequently, the CMIG module extracts modality-specific advantageous features and guides the other modality to enhance the expression of key information, obtaining refined and enhanced feature representations. To reduce computational complexity, we introduce the DCA module, which selects only partial keys and values for each query within a local region of the feature map. Extensive comparative experiments and ablation experiments on multiple public datasets achieve state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method. Published: 2026-05-23T15:07:22+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.807 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Hongtao Wu; Jing Rong; Qiuzhan Zhou; Di Yang; Weiqi Wang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114024

Relevance: 0.807 (must_read)

Multi-spectral object detection gains significant attention due to its potential in all-weather applications, particularly those involving RGB and Infrared (IR) images. However, existing methods fail to account for the semantic differences between the RGB and IR modalities, resulting in insufficient modal feature fusion and limiting the performance of RGB-IR object detection. To address these issues, we propose a novel learning network, the Semantic Alignment Guided Fusion Network (SAGF-Net), that fuses RGB and IR images in a guided manner to extract more complementary feature representations. Our network consists of three key modules: Cross-Scale Semantic Alignment (CSSA), Cross-Modal Information Guidance (CMIG), and Deformable Cross-Attention (DCA). The CSSA module alleviates semantic differences across different receptive fields through cross-scale dynamic attention fusion. Subsequently, the CMIG module extracts modality-specific advantageous features and guides the other modality to enhance the expression of key information, obtaining refined and enhanced feature representations. To reduce computational complexity, we introduce the DCA module, which selects only partial keys and values for each query within a local region of the feature map. Extensive comparative experiments and ablation experiments on multiple public datasets achieve state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: PolSAR vehicle recognition via scattering mechanism-driven hybrid attention
item.dc:creator: Jie Deng
Wei Wang
Huiqiang Zhang
Deliang Xiang
Jun Zhang
item.description: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR), as an advanced remote sensing technology, can provide rich polarization information and is of great significance for target recognition in complex environments. However, deep learning methods based on neural networks often fall into the dilemma of convergence or over-fitting due to insufficient samples and weak physical perception. To address this limitation, we propose a PolSAR vehicle target recognition method via scattering mechanism-driven hybrid attention. Firstly, we introduce a physics-inspired neural module, termed the Polarization Knowledge Extraction Module (PKEM), which retrieves physical scattering information from the Discrete Null-Pol Synthesis Pattern (DNSP). We then propose a Polarization Guided Feature Enhancement (PGFE) module, which dynamically re-weights deep features according to the physical prior information, thereby enhancing the model’s physical perception capabilities. Finally, we constructed a PolSAR vehicle target recognition dataset that can significantly facilitate the advancement of PolSAR ATR. Extensive experimental results on our self-collected measured dataset and Gotcha dataset confirm the superior effectiveness of the proposed physics-inspired target recognition framework. Our data will be available at https://github.com/DJJJJJJ/SMG_PolSAR . Published: 2026-05-23T15:40:00+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.803 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jie Deng; Wei Wang; Huiqiang Zhang; Deliang Xiang; Jun Zhang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114061

Relevance: 0.803 (must_read)

Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR), as an advanced remote sensing technology, can provide rich polarization information and is of great significance for target recognition in complex environments. However, deep learning methods based on neural networks often fall into the dilemma of convergence or over-fitting due to insufficient samples and weak physical perception. To address this limitation, we propose a PolSAR vehicle target recognition method via scattering mechanism-driven hybrid attention. Firstly, we introduce a physics-inspired neural module, termed the Polarization Knowledge Extraction Module (PKEM), which retrieves physical scattering information from the Discrete Null-Pol Synthesis Pattern (DNSP). We then propose a Polarization Guided Feature Enhancement (PGFE) module, which dynamically re-weights deep features according to the physical prior information, thereby enhancing the model’s physical perception capabilities. Finally, we constructed a PolSAR vehicle target recognition dataset that can significantly facilitate the advancement of PolSAR ATR. Extensive experimental results on our self-collected measured dataset and Gotcha dataset confirm the superior effectiveness of the proposed physics-inspired target recognition framework. Our data will be available at https://github.com/DJJJJJJ/SMG_PolSAR .

item.title: XFuse: Multi-sensor CNN transformer fusion with cross attention and quality aware curriculum learning for high resolution fractional snow cover mapping
item.dc:creator: Getnet Demil
Mourad Oussalah
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Accurate fractional snow cover (FSC) mapping requires combining the complementary strengths of optical imagery (spectral discrimination) and Synthetic Aperture Radar (all-weather capability). Yet existing fusion approaches employ simplistic concatenation strategies that fail to capture complex inter-modal relationships, while taking into account the resolution mismatch between high-resolution inputs and coarse reference labels. This paper introduces XFuse , a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture that addresses these challenges through three key contributions: (1) bidirectional cross-attention fusion that explicitly models SAR-optical interdependencies via query-key–value transformations, enabling each modality to selectively attend to complementary information from the other; (2) a multi-resolution training strategy ( 10 m → 60 m → 10 m " role="presentation"> 10 m → 60 m → 10 m 10 m → 60 m → 10 m ) that fuses Sentinel-1/2 features at native resolution, aggregates to 60 m for supervision against Gap-Filled Fractional Snow Cover (GFSC) labels, and disaggregates through guided upsampling to restore fine spatial structure at 10 m " role="presentation"> 10 m 10 m ; and (3) quality-aware curriculum learning that incorporates multi-source metadata (cloud masks, atmospheric parameters, quality control flags) through adaptive loss weighting, enabling robust training from heterogeneous satellite data while providing calibrated uncertainty estimates. Evaluation on 29 Sentinel-1/2 tiles from Northern Finland (18,117 test patches from 4 geographically unseen tiles) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance: 13.77% MAE and 94.66% classification accuracy, representing 38.7% MAE reduction over the best baseline. Ablation studies confirm cross-attention as the largest individual contributor (5.16 percentage point accuracy gain), while uncertainty quantification achieves excellent calibration (ECE=0.025). The methodology generalizes to earth observation applications requiring fusion of heterogeneous multi-resolution satellite data Published: 2026-05-23T00:33:24+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Getnet Demil; Mourad Oussalah

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.028

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Accurate fractional snow cover (FSC) mapping requires combining the complementary strengths of optical imagery (spectral discrimination) and Synthetic Aperture Radar (all-weather capability). Yet existing fusion approaches employ simplistic concatenation strategies that fail to capture complex inter-modal relationships, while taking into account the resolution mismatch between high-resolution inputs and coarse reference labels. This paper introduces XFuse , a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture that addresses these challenges through three key contributions: (1) bidirectional cross-attention fusion that explicitly models SAR-optical interdependencies via query-key–value transformations, enabling each modality to selectively attend to complementary information from the other; (2) a multi-resolution training strategy ( 10 m → 60 m → 10 m " role="presentation"> 10 m → 60 m → 10 m 10 m → 60 m → 10 m ) that fuses Sentinel-1/2 features at native resolution, aggregates to 60 m for supervision against Gap-Filled Fractional Snow Cover (GFSC) labels, and disaggregates through guided upsampling to restore fine spatial structure at 10 m " role="presentation"> 10 m 10 m ; and (3) quality-aware curriculum learning that incorporates multi-source metadata (cloud masks, atmospheric parameters, quality control flags) through adaptive loss weighting, enabling robust training from heterogeneous satellite data while providing calibrated uncertainty estimates. Evaluation on 29 Sentinel-1/2 tiles from Northern Finland (18,117 test patches from 4 geographically unseen tiles) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance: 13.77% MAE and 94.66% classification accuracy, representing 38.7% MAE reduction over the best baseline. Ablation studies confirm cross-attention as the largest individual contributor (5.16 percentage point accuracy gain), while uncertainty quantification achieves excellent calibration (ECE=0.025). The methodology generalizes to earth observation applications requiring fusion of heterogeneous multi-resolution satellite data

item.title: Rank-Aware Routing Decomposition for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
item.dc:creator: Yuanye Liu
Renwei Dian
Zhijun Zhao
Shutao Li
item.description: The low-rank property of hyperspectral images (HSIs) has been widely exploited in hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HMF). Although HSIs typically exhibit the low-rank property, structurally heterogeneous regions with strong spatial–spectral coupling tend to disrupt this characteristic, making them difficult to accurately represent under a finite-rank constraint. However, existing fusion methods, which apply indiscriminate global decomposition, fail to accurately represent these regions, leading to degraded reconstruction quality. To overcome this drawback, we propose a novel Rank-Aware Selective Decomposition (RASD) module, which adaptively assigns different network branches to each feature point based on its low-rank property. Specifically, RASD employs a point-wise routing strategy with a learnable Rank-Aware Discriminator to evaluate the low-rank confidence of each feature point. High-confidence feature points are routed to the lightweight Low-Rank Decomposition Branch, while low-confidence feature points are processed by the high-capacity Efficient Attention Branch. To enhance the representation accuracy of the latter, we design a Local Structure-Enhanced Attention (LSEA) module. It integrates spectral self-attention with Selective State-Space Model (Mamba) to jointly model long-range spatial-spectral dependencies. To mitigate the loss of local information introduced by the routing strategy, the local directional gradient information is explicitly incorporated into the low-confidence feature points. Finally, we develop RASD-FuNet by cascading multiple RASD modules. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that RASD-FuNet surpasses several state-of-the-art methods while requiring significantly fewer floating-point operations (FLOPs). The code is available at https://github.com/YuanyeLiu/Rank-Aware-Routing-Decomposition-for-Hyperspectral-and-Multispectral-Image-Fusion . Published: 2026-05-24T00:42:01+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuanye Liu; Renwei Dian; Zhijun Zhao; Shutao Li

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104498

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

The low-rank property of hyperspectral images (HSIs) has been widely exploited in hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HMF). Although HSIs typically exhibit the low-rank property, structurally heterogeneous regions with strong spatial–spectral coupling tend to disrupt this characteristic, making them difficult to accurately represent under a finite-rank constraint. However, existing fusion methods, which apply indiscriminate global decomposition, fail to accurately represent these regions, leading to degraded reconstruction quality. To overcome this drawback, we propose a novel Rank-Aware Selective Decomposition (RASD) module, which adaptively assigns different network branches to each feature point based on its low-rank property. Specifically, RASD employs a point-wise routing strategy with a learnable Rank-Aware Discriminator to evaluate the low-rank confidence of each feature point. High-confidence feature points are routed to the lightweight Low-Rank Decomposition Branch, while low-confidence feature points are processed by the high-capacity Efficient Attention Branch. To enhance the representation accuracy of the latter, we design a Local Structure-Enhanced Attention (LSEA) module. It integrates spectral self-attention with Selective State-Space Model (Mamba) to jointly model long-range spatial-spectral dependencies. To mitigate the loss of local information introduced by the routing strategy, the local directional gradient information is explicitly incorporated into the low-confidence feature points. Finally, we develop RASD-FuNet by cascading multiple RASD modules. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that RASD-FuNet surpasses several state-of-the-art methods while requiring significantly fewer floating-point operations (FLOPs). The code is available at https://github.com/YuanyeLiu/Rank-Aware-Routing-Decomposition-for-Hyperspectral-and-Multispectral-Image-Fusion .

item.title: Sparse multimodal feature fusion transformer with frequency-convolution collaboration for wildlife multi-object tracking in UAV monitoring
item.dc:creator: Zixuan Yin
Yaqin Zhao
Panpan Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Lightweight unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are gradually becoming important remote sensing tools for wildlife monitoring. Wildlife tracking using UAVs faces challenges, including small targets, highly similar appearances, and strong camouflage in natural environments. To address these issues, we propose a multimodal wildlife multi-object tracking model based on text–image feature fusion. First, the Density Multimodal Fusion (DTF) module combines spatial-density-guided sparse sampling with text priors to focus on high-density foregrounds while suppressing background redundancy. Second, the frequency-convolution collaborative encoder (LST-Encoder) performs spatially conditioned spectral modulation to enhance informative frequency components and preserve global semantics, improving discriminability for camouflaged and visually similar targets. Finally, the Hybrid-Decoder mitigates ID switches and identity fragmentation by integrating global spatiotemporal modeling with hierarchical identity modulation, maintaining cross-frame feature continuity and reinforcing identity-specific cues for stable long-sequence tracking. We validate our approach on the newly constructed MiluDrone dataset and public datasets. The experimental results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms several representative trackers, achieving a 4% increase in MOTA, a 3.7% increase in IDF1, and a 47% reduction in ID switches on the MiluDrone dataset. These results demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed framework for challenging UAV-based wildlife monitoring scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/yzxhao123/De-MOTR.git . Published: 2026-05-23T13:35:46+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zixuan Yin; Yaqin Zhao; Panpan Li

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.033

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

Lightweight unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are gradually becoming important remote sensing tools for wildlife monitoring. Wildlife tracking using UAVs faces challenges, including small targets, highly similar appearances, and strong camouflage in natural environments. To address these issues, we propose a multimodal wildlife multi-object tracking model based on text–image feature fusion. First, the Density Multimodal Fusion (DTF) module combines spatial-density-guided sparse sampling with text priors to focus on high-density foregrounds while suppressing background redundancy. Second, the frequency-convolution collaborative encoder (LST-Encoder) performs spatially conditioned spectral modulation to enhance informative frequency components and preserve global semantics, improving discriminability for camouflaged and visually similar targets. Finally, the Hybrid-Decoder mitigates ID switches and identity fragmentation by integrating global spatiotemporal modeling with hierarchical identity modulation, maintaining cross-frame feature continuity and reinforcing identity-specific cues for stable long-sequence tracking. We validate our approach on the newly constructed MiluDrone dataset and public datasets. The experimental results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms several representative trackers, achieving a 4% increase in MOTA, a 3.7% increase in IDF1, and a 47% reduction in ID switches on the MiluDrone dataset. These results demonstrate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed framework for challenging UAV-based wildlife monitoring scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/yzxhao123/De-MOTR.git .

item.title: Vision Transformers Need Better Token Interaction
item.dc:creator: Linxiang Su
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) can learn strong image-level representations while their patch representations become less effective for dense prediction during prolonged training. We revisit this dense degradation phenomenon and argue that it is not fully explained by high-norm artifacts alone. Instead, we characterize \emph{semantic diffusion}: an optimization shortcut in which global semantic information spreads through patch tokens beyond what is locally justified. Our analysis shows that dense representation quality is not captured by locality alone: shallow features can remain better aligned with foreground regions yet underperform deeper features, and \texttt{[CLS]} features remain complementary for dense prediction. These observations suggest that the goal should not be to remove global context, but to make token interactions more selective. We therefore study sparse attention as a minimal intervention, replacing softmax attention with entmax-1.5 while preserving global token connectivity. On DINOv1 ViT-S/16 trained for 200 epochs on ImageNet-1K, this change preserves ImageNet linear probing accuracy and substantially improves semantic segmentation performance: VOC mIoU increases from 42.80 to 48.78, ADE20K from 19.85 to 21.97, and Cityscapes from 36.79 to 37.87. These results suggest that selective token mixing is a simple and effective bias for improving dense ViT representations. Published: 2026-05-22T17:25:16+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Linxiang Su

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Vision Transformers (ViTs) can learn strong image-level representations while their patch representations become less effective for dense prediction during prolonged training. We revisit this dense degradation phenomenon and argue that it is not fully explained by high-norm artifacts alone. Instead, we characterize \emph{semantic diffusion}: an optimization shortcut in which global semantic information spreads through patch tokens beyond what is locally justified. Our analysis shows that dense representation quality is not captured by locality alone: shallow features can remain better aligned with foreground regions yet underperform deeper features, and \texttt{[CLS]} features remain complementary for dense prediction. These observations suggest that the goal should not be to remove global context, but to make token interactions more selective. We therefore study sparse attention as a minimal intervention, replacing softmax attention with entmax-1.5 while preserving global token connectivity. On DINOv1 ViT-S/16 trained for 200 epochs on ImageNet-1K, this change preserves ImageNet linear probing accuracy and substantially improves semantic segmentation performance: VOC mIoU increases from 42.80 to 48.78, ADE20K from 19.85 to 21.97, and Cityscapes from 36.79 to 37.87. These results suggest that selective token mixing is a simple and effective bias for improving dense ViT representations.

item.title: Unified skeleton representation learning for autonomous driving
item.dc:creator: Mohamed Abdelfattah
Alexandre Alahi
item.prism:publicationName: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies
item.description: How can a model learn human skeleton representations that generalize across multiple tasks in autonomous driving and intelligent transportation systems? Traditional self-supervised methods fall short by entangling spatial and temporal features, limiting their adaptability to downstream perception and behavior understanding tasks. In this paper, we introduce MotionMoCo , a novel self-supervised framework designed to decouple spatial and temporal information for robust, transferable skeleton representations. MotionMoCo consists of two branches, a query branch and a momentum-updated key branch, each of which separately encodes the spatial, temporal, and combined features from the input motion sequence. Unlike previous works, we contrast these features independently across the two branches, thus preserving their complementary information content. Further, we propose a Motion-Aware Frame Masking strategy that selectively removes fast-evolving frames in the query branch to encourage robust feature extraction. MotionMoCo serves as a generic pretraining stage that can be fine-tuned for a range of autonomous driving applications, including pedestrian intention prediction, skeleton-based action recognition, 2D-to-3D pose lifting, and human mesh recovery, demonstrating that spatiotemporal disentanglement enhances the quality and adaptability of skeleton representations. Published: 2026-05-23T00:02:22+00:00 Venue: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mohamed Abdelfattah; Alexandre Alahi

Journal: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies

DOI: 10.1016/j.trc.2026.105753

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

How can a model learn human skeleton representations that generalize across multiple tasks in autonomous driving and intelligent transportation systems? Traditional self-supervised methods fall short by entangling spatial and temporal features, limiting their adaptability to downstream perception and behavior understanding tasks. In this paper, we introduce MotionMoCo , a novel self-supervised framework designed to decouple spatial and temporal information for robust, transferable skeleton representations. MotionMoCo consists of two branches, a query branch and a momentum-updated key branch, each of which separately encodes the spatial, temporal, and combined features from the input motion sequence. Unlike previous works, we contrast these features independently across the two branches, thus preserving their complementary information content. Further, we propose a Motion-Aware Frame Masking strategy that selectively removes fast-evolving frames in the query branch to encourage robust feature extraction. MotionMoCo serves as a generic pretraining stage that can be fine-tuned for a range of autonomous driving applications, including pedestrian intention prediction, skeleton-based action recognition, 2D-to-3D pose lifting, and human mesh recovery, demonstrating that spatiotemporal disentanglement enhances the quality and adaptability of skeleton representations.

item.title: Deviation-guided dual-model machine learning SAR coherence change detection framework for extracting changes in low-coherence areas
item.dc:creator: Dayuan Liu
Timo Balz
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Coherence Change Detection (CCD) is a crucial technique for monitoring surface dynamics in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), and it has wide-ranging applications in natural disaster monitoring and urban analysis. However, conventional CCD methods based on dual-temporal coherence typically rely on a single coherence estimate for decision-making. This makes it difficult to effectively distinguish between decoherence caused by real surface changes and that induced by other factors, such as geometric decorrelation, temporal decorrelation, system noise, and co-registration inaccuracies. To address this issue, this study proposes a novel dual-model framework for coherence change detection that integrates machine learning with coherence and deviation modeling. The proposed framework first employs a machine learning (ML) model for coherence estimation, followed by a deviation prediction model that learns the mapping between statistical features and estimation errors. The SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method is then applied to interpret the deviation model. Based on the joint output of coherence and deviation estimates, a dual-threshold segmentation strategy is adopted to effectively extract low-coherence regions caused by genuine surface changes. Experimental results confirm the method’s robustness in small-sample and low-coherence scenarios. Validation with real-world data across different resolutions and scenarios confirms the method’s ability to accurately identify low-coherence regions caused by changes. By jointly utilizing coherence and deviation information, the proposed framework enhances detection accuracy and interpretability, providing a robust and reliable new CCD solution. Published: 2026-05-23T16:47:43+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.782 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dayuan Liu; Timo Balz

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.034

Relevance: 0.782 (must_read)

Coherence Change Detection (CCD) is a crucial technique for monitoring surface dynamics in Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), and it has wide-ranging applications in natural disaster monitoring and urban analysis. However, conventional CCD methods based on dual-temporal coherence typically rely on a single coherence estimate for decision-making. This makes it difficult to effectively distinguish between decoherence caused by real surface changes and that induced by other factors, such as geometric decorrelation, temporal decorrelation, system noise, and co-registration inaccuracies. To address this issue, this study proposes a novel dual-model framework for coherence change detection that integrates machine learning with coherence and deviation modeling. The proposed framework first employs a machine learning (ML) model for coherence estimation, followed by a deviation prediction model that learns the mapping between statistical features and estimation errors. The SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method is then applied to interpret the deviation model. Based on the joint output of coherence and deviation estimates, a dual-threshold segmentation strategy is adopted to effectively extract low-coherence regions caused by genuine surface changes. Experimental results confirm the method’s robustness in small-sample and low-coherence scenarios. Validation with real-world data across different resolutions and scenarios confirms the method’s ability to accurately identify low-coherence regions caused by changes. By jointly utilizing coherence and deviation information, the proposed framework enhances detection accuracy and interpretability, providing a robust and reliable new CCD solution.

item.title: TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?
item.dc:creator: Tobias Christian Nauen
Stanislav Frolov
Brian Bernhard Moser
Federico Raue
Ahmed Anwar
Andreas Dengel
item.description: The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models. Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher Published: 2026-05-21T07:36:00+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tobias Christian Nauen; Stanislav Frolov; Brian Bernhard Moser; Federico Raue; Ahmed Anwar; Andreas Dengel

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models. Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher

item.title: Accelerating Vision Foundation Models with Drop-in Depthwise Convolution
item.dc:creator: Carmelo Scribano
Mohammad Mahdi
Nedyalko Prisadnikov
Yuqian Fu
Giorgia Franchini
Danda Pani Paudel
Marko Bertogna
Luc Van Gool
item.description: Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available. Published: 2026-05-21T08:07:23+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Carmelo Scribano; Mohammad Mahdi; Nedyalko Prisadnikov; Yuqian Fu; Giorgia Franchini; Danda Pani Paudel; Marko Bertogna; Luc Van Gool

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available.

item.title: Supervised Classification Heads as Semantic Prototypes: Unlocking Vision-Language Alignment via Weight Recycling
item.dc:creator: David Méndez
Roberto Confalonieri
Natalia Díaz Rodríguez
item.description: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at tasks like zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval by mapping images and text to a shared space, but this requires expensive end-to-end training with massive paired datasets. Current post-hoc alignment methods reduce computational costs by connecting pretrained encoders through lightweight mappings, yet still demand substantial paired data. In this work, we investigate the potential of repurposing the classification heads of pretrained vision models as semantic prototypes. The recycling of these weights, typically discarded after pretraining, unlocks two distinct capabilities: it enables zero-shot alignment by using weights as semantic anchors, and serves as a robust data augmentation strategy by mixing these prototypes with real image-text pairs. We demonstrate that integrating our approach with several state-of-the-art post-hoc alignment techniques consistently boosts accuracy in cross-modal retrieval, zero- and few-shot classification tasks. Published: 2026-05-21T13:41:18+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: David Méndez; Roberto Confalonieri; Natalia Díaz Rodríguez

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at tasks like zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval by mapping images and text to a shared space, but this requires expensive end-to-end training with massive paired datasets. Current post-hoc alignment methods reduce computational costs by connecting pretrained encoders through lightweight mappings, yet still demand substantial paired data. In this work, we investigate the potential of repurposing the classification heads of pretrained vision models as semantic prototypes. The recycling of these weights, typically discarded after pretraining, unlocks two distinct capabilities: it enables zero-shot alignment by using weights as semantic anchors, and serves as a robust data augmentation strategy by mixing these prototypes with real image-text pairs. We demonstrate that integrating our approach with several state-of-the-art post-hoc alignment techniques consistently boosts accuracy in cross-modal retrieval, zero- and few-shot classification tasks.

item.title: Bidirectional token-masking autoencoder for Referring Image Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Minhyeok Lee
Dogyoon Lee
Jungho Lee
Suhwan Cho
Sangyoun Lee
item.description: Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment target objects expressed in natural language within a scene at the pixel level. Various recent RIS models have achieved state-of-the-art performance by generating contextual tokens to model multimodal features from pretrained encoders and effectively fusing them using transformer-based cross-modal attention. While these methods match language features with image features to effectively identify likely target objects, they often struggle to correctly understand contextual information in complex and ambiguous sentences and scenes. To address this issue, we propose a novel bidirectional token-masking autoencoder (BTMAE) inspired by the masked autoencoder (MAE). The proposed model learns the context of image-to-language and language-to-image by reconstructing missing features in both image and language features at the token level. In other words, this approach involves mutually complementing across the features of images and language, with a focus on enabling the network to understand interconnected deep contextual information between the two modalities. This learning method enhances the robustness of RIS performance in complex sentences and scenes. BTMAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on three popular datasets. Published: 2026-05-23T06:46:47+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.772 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Minhyeok Lee; Dogyoon Lee; Jungho Lee; Suhwan Cho; Sangyoun Lee

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114006

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) aims to segment target objects expressed in natural language within a scene at the pixel level. Various recent RIS models have achieved state-of-the-art performance by generating contextual tokens to model multimodal features from pretrained encoders and effectively fusing them using transformer-based cross-modal attention. While these methods match language features with image features to effectively identify likely target objects, they often struggle to correctly understand contextual information in complex and ambiguous sentences and scenes. To address this issue, we propose a novel bidirectional token-masking autoencoder (BTMAE) inspired by the masked autoencoder (MAE). The proposed model learns the context of image-to-language and language-to-image by reconstructing missing features in both image and language features at the token level. In other words, this approach involves mutually complementing across the features of images and language, with a focus on enabling the network to understand interconnected deep contextual information between the two modalities. This learning method enhances the robustness of RIS performance in complex sentences and scenes. BTMAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on three popular datasets.

item.title: ASAP: Attention Sink Anchored Pruning
item.dc:creator: Jaehyuk Lee
Hanyoung Kim
Yanggee Kim
Donghun Lee
item.description: Vision Transformers (ViTs) face severe computational bottlenecks due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention at high resolutions. Existing token reduction methods rely on local metrics - such as single-layer attention scores - that are inherently vulnerable to the attention sink phenomenon, where uninformative tokens are paradoxically preserved over salient foreground objects. We propose ASAP (Attention Sink Anchored Pruning), a training-free framework that recasts this sink as a feature. Modeling ViT information flow as a Lazy Random Walk, ASAP identifies the sink as a dominant accumulator of probability mass. By computing the diffusion distance to the sink within the cumulative transition matrix, ASAP partitions tokens via Radial Diffusion Clustering and compresses background redundancy through Transition Weight Pooling in a single shot. Extensive experiments across image, video, and vision-language tasks demonstrate ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art methods, accelerating throughput by up to 48% while maintaining - or even exceeding - baseline accuracy. Published: 2026-05-21T12:04:49+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.772 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jaehyuk Lee; Hanyoung Kim; Yanggee Kim; Donghun Lee

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Vision Transformers (ViTs) face severe computational bottlenecks due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention at high resolutions. Existing token reduction methods rely on local metrics - such as single-layer attention scores - that are inherently vulnerable to the attention sink phenomenon, where uninformative tokens are paradoxically preserved over salient foreground objects. We propose ASAP (Attention Sink Anchored Pruning), a training-free framework that recasts this sink as a feature. Modeling ViT information flow as a Lazy Random Walk, ASAP identifies the sink as a dominant accumulator of probability mass. By computing the diffusion distance to the sink within the cumulative transition matrix, ASAP partitions tokens via Radial Diffusion Clustering and compresses background redundancy through Transition Weight Pooling in a single shot. Extensive experiments across image, video, and vision-language tasks demonstrate ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art methods, accelerating throughput by up to 48% while maintaining - or even exceeding - baseline accuracy.

item.title: MemGAN: Hallucinating robust knowledge memorized in pre-trained GANs latent space for few-shot image generation
item.dc:creator: Wenkuan Li
Ming Li
Haifang Li
item.description: Few-shot image generation aims to synthesize diverse images for new unseen classes with limited conditional inputs. To make full use of limited conditional data per class, the prevalent paradigm is to fuse the representation vectors of conditional images according to their semantic similarity in the embedding space. However, when only one sample each unseen class is available, this fusion-based strategy will fall into mode collapse where the synthetic images exhibit a single pattern. In this paper, we propose a novel finetune-based generation paradigm to alleviate the one-shot dilemma by exploiting prior knowledge memorized in pre-trained GANs latent space as an auxiliary reference, dubbed MemGAN. Specifically, we first pre-train GANs with a generic image-to-image translation task on the training set to encode generalizable knowledge into its latent space and explore both maximum intra-class margins and base-class embeddings. A closed-form disentanglement is developed to explicitly decompose reusable prior knowledge of pre-trained latent space via aligning with the conditional input at the same semantic space, refining prior knowledge as a reliable reference. A learnable uncertainty-aware mixup is finetuned to adaptively integrate the disentanglement knowledge with a conditional input at different levels of the latent space, promoting the intra-class diversity and base-class consistency of mixup generation references. With robust auxiliary reference to stimulate pre-trained generator, MemGAN could dispersively push a conditional point to various points at the same class space, so as to generate diverse images at the one-shot case. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of MemGAN at one-shot setting without additional test-time adaptation. Published: 2026-05-23T15:43:46+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.772 (must_read)

Authors: Wenkuan Li; Ming Li; Haifang Li

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116217

Relevance: 0.772 (must_read)

Few-shot image generation aims to synthesize diverse images for new unseen classes with limited conditional inputs. To make full use of limited conditional data per class, the prevalent paradigm is to fuse the representation vectors of conditional images according to their semantic similarity in the embedding space. However, when only one sample each unseen class is available, this fusion-based strategy will fall into mode collapse where the synthetic images exhibit a single pattern. In this paper, we propose a novel finetune-based generation paradigm to alleviate the one-shot dilemma by exploiting prior knowledge memorized in pre-trained GANs latent space as an auxiliary reference, dubbed MemGAN. Specifically, we first pre-train GANs with a generic image-to-image translation task on the training set to encode generalizable knowledge into its latent space and explore both maximum intra-class margins and base-class embeddings. A closed-form disentanglement is developed to explicitly decompose reusable prior knowledge of pre-trained latent space via aligning with the conditional input at the same semantic space, refining prior knowledge as a reliable reference. A learnable uncertainty-aware mixup is finetuned to adaptively integrate the disentanglement knowledge with a conditional input at different levels of the latent space, promoting the intra-class diversity and base-class consistency of mixup generation references. With robust auxiliary reference to stimulate pre-trained generator, MemGAN could dispersively push a conditional point to various points at the same class space, so as to generate diverse images at the one-shot case. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of MemGAN at one-shot setting without additional test-time adaptation.

item.title: SLIP-RS: Structured-Attribute Language-Image Pre-Training for Remote Sensing Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Chenxu Wang
Yuxuan Li
Yunheng Li
Xiang Li
Jingyuan Xia
Qibin Hou
item.description: Existing language-image pre-training for remote sensing object detection is constrained by Monolithic Label Learning, which relies on exhaustively enumerating open-set categories via black-box data to acquire fine-grained representations, creating a dependency incompatible with the domain's inherent data scarcity. To transcend this bottleneck, we propose SLIP-RS, establishing a Structured-Attribute Decoupling Paradigm that maps the open-ended category space into a finite, physically meaningful attribute space, unlocking fine-grained discriminability via explicit structural logic. This paradigm is realized via two technical pillars: (1) Structured-Attribute Contrastive Learning, which enforces the learning of decoupled intrinsic visual logic via combinatorial attribute augmentation; and (2) Conformal Attribute Reliability Engine, which leverages conformal prediction theory to rigorously distill high-fidelity supervision from noisy sources, yielding RS-Attribute-15M, the largest dataset with over 15 million attribute annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLIP-RS establishes unprecedented performance in fine-grained detection and cross-domain generalization, validating structured attributes as a vital foundation for remote sensing. Code: https://github.com/facias914/SLIP-RS. Published: 2026-05-22T01:50:19+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.771 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chenxu Wang; Yuxuan Li; Yunheng Li; Xiang Li; Jingyuan Xia; Qibin Hou

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.771 (must_read)

Existing language-image pre-training for remote sensing object detection is constrained by Monolithic Label Learning, which relies on exhaustively enumerating open-set categories via black-box data to acquire fine-grained representations, creating a dependency incompatible with the domain's inherent data scarcity. To transcend this bottleneck, we propose SLIP-RS, establishing a Structured-Attribute Decoupling Paradigm that maps the open-ended category space into a finite, physically meaningful attribute space, unlocking fine-grained discriminability via explicit structural logic. This paradigm is realized via two technical pillars: (1) Structured-Attribute Contrastive Learning, which enforces the learning of decoupled intrinsic visual logic via combinatorial attribute augmentation; and (2) Conformal Attribute Reliability Engine, which leverages conformal prediction theory to rigorously distill high-fidelity supervision from noisy sources, yielding RS-Attribute-15M, the largest dataset with over 15 million attribute annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLIP-RS establishes unprecedented performance in fine-grained detection and cross-domain generalization, validating structured attributes as a vital foundation for remote sensing. Code: https://github.com/facias914/SLIP-RS.

item.title: MXPose: Multiplex interactive learning for multi-view 3D Human Pose Estimation
item.dc:creator: Wanruo Zhang
Zhou Guan
Mengyuan Liu
Bin Ren
Hong Liu
item.description: Multi-view 3D Human Pose Estimation (HPE) effectively alleviates occlusion and depth ambiguity. However, existing approaches often rely on precise camera calibration or monolithic modeling strategies, limiting robustness in complex scenarios. In this paper, MXPose is presented for multi-view 3D human pose estimation, which is a multiplex interactive learning framework. This framework models interactions across spatial structure, view-wise geometry, temporal dynamics, and optimization stages. Specifically, a Multiplex Interactive Spatial Encoder (MXS) is designed to realize spatial multiplex interaction by jointly modeling joint-level, part-level, and body-level representations through hierarchical dynamic hypergraphs. To facilitate view-wise interaction, a Cross-View Fusion (CVF) module is utilized to aggregate multi-view information into a unified geometric representation. Furthermore, a Multiplex Interactive Temporal Module (MXT) is introduced to integrate long-range temporal attention with adaptive multi-scale convolution and high-order kinematic cues. Finally, a Progressive Multi-Stage Supervision (PMSS) strategy is employed to enable interaction among geometric, structural, and projective objectives, guiding stable optimization from coarse initialization to refined alignment. Extensive experiments on Human3.6M, MPI-INF-3DHP, and Ski-Pose benchmarks demonstrate that MXPose consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, validating its robustness in challenging, uncalibrated multi-view settings. Code is available at: https://github.com/Jokerealm/MXPose . Published: 2026-05-23T15:07:34+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.771 (must_read)

Furthermore, a Multiplex Interactive Temporal Module (MXT) is introduced to integrate long-range temporal attention with adaptive multi-scale convolution and high-order kinematic cues. Finally, a Progressive Multi-Stage Supervision (PMSS) strategy is employed to enable interaction among geometric, structural, and projective objectives, guiding stable optimization from coarse initialization to refined alignment. Extensive experiments on Human3.6M, MPI-INF-3DHP, and Ski-Pose benchmarks demonstrate that MXPose consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, validating its robustness in challenging, uncalibrated multi-view settings. Code is available at: https://github.com/Jokerealm/MXPose .

item.title: DOEI: Dual optimization of embedding information for attention-enhanced class activation maps
item.dc:creator: Hongjie Zhu
Zeyu Zhang
Huazhang Wang
Xu Wang
Shimin Wen
Yu Bai
Yuxuan Chen
Daji Ergu
Ying Cai
Yang Zhao
item.description: Weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) typically utilizes limited semantic annotations to obtain initial Class Activation Maps (CAMs). However, due to the inadequate coupling between class activation responses and semantic information in high-dimensional space, the CAM is prone to object co-occurrence or under-activation, resulting in inferior recognition accuracy. To tackle this issue, we propose DOEI , D ual O ptimization of E mbedding I nformation, a novel approach that reconstructs embedding representations through semantic-aware attention weight matrices to optimize the expression capability of embedding information. Specifically, DOEI amplifies tokens with high confidence and suppresses those with low confidence during the class-to-patch interaction. This alignment of activation responses with semantic information strengthens the propagation and decoupling of target features, enabling the generated embeddings to more accurately represent target features in high-level semantic space. In addition, we propose a hybrid-feature alignment module in DOEI that combines RGB values, embedding-guided features, and self-attention weights to increase the reliability of candidate tokens. Comprehensive experiments show that DOEI outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, producing higher-quality CAMs and significantly improving segmentation performance, including PASCAL VOC (79.1% mIoU) and MS COCO (51.1% mIoU). Published: 2026-05-23T15:13:38+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.769 (must_read)

Authors: Hongjie Zhu; Zeyu Zhang; Huazhang Wang; Xu Wang; Shimin Wen; Yu Bai; Yuxuan Chen; Daji Ergu; Ying Cai; Yang Zhao

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134084

Relevance: 0.769 (must_read)

Weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) typically utilizes limited semantic annotations to obtain initial Class Activation Maps (CAMs). However, due to the inadequate coupling between class activation responses and semantic information in high-dimensional space, the CAM is prone to object co-occurrence or under-activation, resulting in inferior recognition accuracy. To tackle this issue, we propose DOEI , D ual O ptimization of E mbedding I nformation, a novel approach that reconstructs embedding representations through semantic-aware attention weight matrices to optimize the expression capability of embedding information. Specifically, DOEI amplifies tokens with high confidence and suppresses those with low confidence during the class-to-patch interaction. This alignment of activation responses with semantic information strengthens the propagation and decoupling of target features, enabling the generated embeddings to more accurately represent target features in high-level semantic space. In addition, we propose a hybrid-feature alignment module in DOEI that combines RGB values, embedding-guided features, and self-attention weights to increase the reliability of candidate tokens. Comprehensive experiments show that DOEI outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, producing higher-quality CAMs and significantly improving segmentation performance, including PASCAL VOC (79.1% mIoU) and MS COCO (51.1% mIoU).

item.title: Multimodal Distribution Matching for Vision-Language Dataset Distillation
item.dc:creator: Jongoh Jeong
Hoyong Kwon
Minseok Kim
Kuk-Jin Yoon
item.description: Dataset distillation compresses large training sets into compact synthetic datasets while preserving downstream performance. As modern systems increasingly operate on paired vision-language inputs, multimodal distillation must preserve representation quality and cross-modal alignment under tight compute and memory budgets, yet prior methods often require heavy computes and overlook their correlations. To address this, we present Multimodal Distribution Matching (MDM), a geometry-aware framework for efficient and generalizable multimodal distillation. Specifically, MDM integrates complementary components at the data, model, and loss levels. At the data level, it initializes synthetic image-text pairs by sampling from clusters in the joint embedding space. At the model level, it forms a mixed teacher by interpolating independently fine-tuned models in weight space according to their angular deviation from the pretrained anchor. At the loss level, it matches joint distributions on the unit hypersphere using a geometry-aware matching objective that exploits the joint features in the cross-modal agreement and discrepancy directions along with symmetric contrastive learning. Across image-text retrieval benchmarks with cross-architecture evaluation, MDM yields compact synthetic sets that preserve multimodal semantics, substantially reduce distillation cost, and remain robust across architectures. Published: 2026-05-22T10:41:58+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.769 (must_read)

Authors: Jongoh Jeong; Hoyong Kwon; Minseok Kim; Kuk-Jin Yoon

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.769 (must_read)

Dataset distillation compresses large training sets into compact synthetic datasets while preserving downstream performance. As modern systems increasingly operate on paired vision-language inputs, multimodal distillation must preserve representation quality and cross-modal alignment under tight compute and memory budgets, yet prior methods often require heavy computes and overlook their correlations. To address this, we present Multimodal Distribution Matching (MDM), a geometry-aware framework for efficient and generalizable multimodal distillation. Specifically, MDM integrates complementary components at the data, model, and loss levels. At the data level, it initializes synthetic image-text pairs by sampling from clusters in the joint embedding space. At the model level, it forms a mixed teacher by interpolating independently fine-tuned models in weight space according to their angular deviation from the pretrained anchor. At the loss level, it matches joint distributions on the unit hypersphere using a geometry-aware matching objective that exploits the joint features in the cross-modal agreement and discrepancy directions along with symmetric contrastive learning. Across image-text retrieval benchmarks with cross-architecture evaluation, MDM yields compact synthetic sets that preserve multimodal semantics, substantially reduce distillation cost, and remain robust across architectures.

item.title: Scene Reconstruction as Mapping Priors for 3D Detection
item.dc:creator: Yang Fu
Yuliang Zou
Hao Xiang
Xin Huang
Yijing Bai
Chen Song
Weijing Shi
Govind Thattai
Dragomir Anguelov
Mingxing Tan
Yingwei Li
item.description: In autonomous driving, mapping is critical for motion planning but remains an under-utilized resource for perception tasks such as 3D object detection. Maps can provide robust structural priors of the static environment, helping resolve ambiguities and correct for sensor data sparsity or noise, especially for distant objects or under adverse weather conditions. However, conventional High-Definition (HD) maps are resource-intensive to obtain and maintain, which presents a challenge for efficient, large-scale deployment. In this paper, we propose a scalable solution to systematically leverage mapping to improve 3D detection by overcoming two primary challenges. First, we introduce a pipeline to automatically build dense mapping priors from aggregated sensor data, eliminating the need for human labeling. Second, we design a novel Mapping Priors Augmented 3D Detection (MPA3D) framework to effectively integrate mapping priors with different sensor modalities. Extensive experiments on the Waymo Open Dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves new state-of-the-art results, proving the effectiveness of scalable reconstructed scene priors for enhancing 3D detection. Published: 2026-05-21T19:52:22+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.769 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yang Fu; Yuliang Zou; Hao Xiang; Xin Huang; Yijing Bai; Chen Song; Weijing Shi; Govind Thattai; Dragomir Anguelov; Mingxing Tan; Yingwei Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.769 (must_read)

In autonomous driving, mapping is critical for motion planning but remains an under-utilized resource for perception tasks such as 3D object detection. Maps can provide robust structural priors of the static environment, helping resolve ambiguities and correct for sensor data sparsity or noise, especially for distant objects or under adverse weather conditions. However, conventional High-Definition (HD) maps are resource-intensive to obtain and maintain, which presents a challenge for efficient, large-scale deployment. In this paper, we propose a scalable solution to systematically leverage mapping to improve 3D detection by overcoming two primary challenges. First, we introduce a pipeline to automatically build dense mapping priors from aggregated sensor data, eliminating the need for human labeling. Second, we design a novel Mapping Priors Augmented 3D Detection (MPA3D) framework to effectively integrate mapping priors with different sensor modalities. Extensive experiments on the Waymo Open Dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves new state-of-the-art results, proving the effectiveness of scalable reconstructed scene priors for enhancing 3D detection.

item.title: Enhancing weakly supervised 3D medical image segmentation through probabilistic-aware Learning
item.dc:creator: Runmin Jiang
Zhaoxin Fan
Junhao Wu
Lenghan Zhu
Xin Huang
Tianyang Wang
Heng Huang
Min Xu
item.description: 3D medical image segmentation is a challenging task with crucial implications for disease diagnosis and treatment planning. Recent advances in deep learning have significantly enhanced fully supervised medical image segmentation. However, this approach heavily relies on labor-intensive and time-consuming fully annotated ground-truth labels, particularly for 3D volumes. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel probabilistic-aware weakly supervised learning pipeline, specifically designed for 3D medical imaging. Our pipeline integrates three innovative components: a Probability-based Pseudo-Label Generation technique for synthesizing dense segmentation masks from sparse annotations, a Probabilistic Multi-head Self-Attention network for robust feature extraction within our Probabilistic Transformer Network, and a Probability-informed Segmentation Loss Function to enhance training with annotation confidence. Demonstrating significant advances, our approach not only rivals the performance of fully supervised methods but also surpasses existing weakly supervised methods in CT and MRI datasets, achieving up to 18.1% improvement in Dice scores for certain organs. The code is available at https://github.com/runminjiang/PW4MedSeg . Published: 2026-05-23T06:46:34+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.767 (must_read)
